play_commands.title: Exploring: Abel Selaocoe
play_commands.narrative: We've already explored Selaocoe's albums and his Giovanni Sollima and Yo-Yo Ma connections — this time let's follow the arranger thread. The Bach Sarabande you're hearing was arranged by Fred Thomas, a British multi-instrumentalist and ECM recording artist who has created bespoke arrangements for Selaocoe, Rachel Podger, and Hilary Hahn. Thomas's own Bach album Three or One reimagines organ chorale preludes for piano trio with cellist Lucy Railton — produced by Manfred Eicher for ECM — offering a fascinating parallel to Selaocoe's cross-cultural reimagining. Meanwhile, Manchester Collective, the string ensemble backing Selaocoe on this very track, recently premiered Hania Rani's piano concerto Non Fiction, and Selaocoe's regular percussion partner Bernhard Schimpelsberger has his own solo album Avenues blending Austrian tabla with electronic textures.

suggestions.label: Bach, JS: Cello Suite No. 6 in D Major, BWV 1012: IV. Sarabande · Abel Selaocoe
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: 🎛️ J.S. Bach: Three or One (2021) — Fred Thomas, Orazbayeva & Railton
suggestions.note: The arranger of Selaocoe's Sarabande has his own ECM Bach album — organ chorale preludes transcribed for piano, violin, and cello, produced by Manfred Eicher. Same reimagining spirit, different cultural lens.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Non Fiction — Piano Concerto (2025) — Hania Rani & Manchester Collective
suggestions.note: Manchester Collective — the same string ensemble backing Selaocoe on this Sarabande — performs Polish pianist Hania Rani's genre-defying piano concerto with electronics and saxophone.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Avenues (2019) — Bernhard Schimpelsberger
suggestions.note: Selaocoe's regular percussion collaborator (they perform together as Chesaba) — Austrian-born tabla player who studied in India, blending Hindustani rhythms with electronic production.

suggestions.label: 💿 Emmanuele (2024) — Abel Selaocoe & Manchester Collective
suggestions.note: A standalone single released ahead of Hymns of Bantu — Selaocoe's original composition with Manchester Collective strings, bridging South African hymn tradition with chamber music.

suggestions.label: 🎵 Song (2022) — Sheku Kanneh-Mason
suggestions.note: Fellow boundary-crossing Black British cellist — where Selaocoe bridges Africa and Baroque, Kanneh-Mason reimagines Fauré, Barber, and Bloch alongside original songs. Both trained at UK conservatoires.

suggestions.label: 🎵 Elgar: Cello Concerto (2020) — Sheku Kanneh-Mason & LSO
suggestions.note: Kanneh-Mason's landmark Elgar Cello Concerto with Simon Rattle and the LSO — the concerto that made him a household name after the Royal Wedding. Includes Bloch and Britten cello works.

play_commands.title: Exploring: Abel Selaocoe
play_commands.narrative: Your first encounter with Abel Selaocoe — a South African cellist, vocalist, and composer who fuses Bantu throat singing and African percussion with Baroque cello repertoire. This Bach Sarabande arrangement (by Fred Thomas for cello & string orchestra) comes from his sophomore album *Hymns of Bantu* (2025, Warner Classics), where it sits alongside traditional South African hymns and Giovanni Sollima's *LBFiles* suite. Selaocoe trained at the Royal Northern College of Music in Manchester and collaborates regularly with Manchester Collective, percussionist Bernhard Schimpelsberger, and djembe player Sidiki Dembélé — and Yo-Yo Ma guests on his debut album *Where Is Home*.

suggestions.label: Bach, JS: Cello Suite No. 6 in D Major, BWV 1012: IV. Sarabande · Abel Selaocoe
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: 💿 Hymns of Bantu (2025) — Abel Selaocoe
suggestions.note: The full album this Sarabande comes from — 12 tracks weaving Bach and Marais with South African hymns, featuring Manchester Collective strings, Fred Thomas on prepared piano, and Dudù Kouaté on African percussion.

suggestions.label: 💿 Where Is Home / Hae ke Kae (2022) — Abel Selaocoe
suggestions.note: Selaocoe's acclaimed debut on Warner Classics — features a collaboration with Yo-Yo Ma on 'Ibuyile I'Africa' and named one of the Best Albums of 2022 by Songlines magazine.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Abel Selaocoe's "Four Spirits" (Live) (2025) — Abel Selaocoe, Aurora Orchestra & Bernhard Schimpelsberger
suggestions.note: Live recording with Aurora Orchestra and conductor Nicholas Collon — Selaocoe's improvising percussionist Bernhard Schimpelsberger joins for a fusion of orchestral and world music traditions.

suggestions.label: 🎛️ We Were Trees (2008) — Giovanni Sollima
suggestions.note: The Sicilian cellist-composer who wrote the 'LBFiles' suite that Selaocoe performs on this EP — Sollima is a boundary-crossing cellist in his own right, blending classical with Mediterranean folk and improvisation.

suggestions.label: 🎛️ J.S. Bach: Cello Suites (2024) — Giovanni Sollima
suggestions.note: Sollima's own reading of the complete Bach Cello Suites — a fascinating comparison with Selaocoe's reimagined Sarabande arrangement. Same repertoire, different cultural lens.

suggestions.label: 📻 Abel Selaocoe — The Complete Playlist (Warner Classics)
suggestions.note: Official Warner Classics playlist spanning Selaocoe's full catalog — 35 tracks including both albums and singles, curated by the label.

play_commands.title: Exploring: Cleo Sol
play_commands.narrative: Cleo Sol's Rose in the Dark is where it all begins — her 2020 debut, entirely produced by her husband Inflo (Dean Josiah Cover), who also helms the mysterious UK collective SAULT and produced Michael Kiwanuka's Mercury Prize-winning KIWANUKA. Every Cleo Sol album lives on Inflo's Forever Living Originals label, making their partnership one of the most prolific artist-producer marriages in modern British soul. She's also featured on two Little Simz albums — "Selfish" on Grey Area (2019) and "Woman" on Sometimes I Might Be Introvert (2021), both also produced by Inflo — a triangle of London talent that keeps feeding itself.

suggestions.label: Rose in the Dark · Cleo Sol
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: 💿 Rose in the Dark (2020) — Cleo Sol
suggestions.note: The album this track is from — her debut, all 11 tracks produced by Inflo. Play the whole thing.

suggestions.label: 🎛️ Mother (2021) — Cleo Sol
suggestions.note: Her second album, also produced by Inflo, inspired by the birth of their child — intimate soul-jazz that deepens everything Rose in the Dark started.

suggestions.label: 🎛️ KIWANUKA (2019) — Michael Kiwanuka
suggestions.note: Produced by Inflo, same as all Cleo Sol albums — won the Mercury Prize. If you love Inflo's warm, expansive production, this is essential.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Sometimes I Might Be Introvert (2021) — Little Simz
suggestions.note: Cleo Sol features on "Woman" and Inflo produced the entire album — the same creative family behind Rose in the Dark.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Untitled (Black Is) (2020) — SAULT
suggestions.note: Cleo Sol is the lead vocalist of SAULT, the secretive collective she runs with Inflo — this critically acclaimed album dropped the same year as Rose in the Dark.

suggestions.label: 💿 Heaven (2023) — Cleo Sol
suggestions.note: Her surprise-released third solo album, continuing the Inflo-produced lineage into more devotional, spiritually-rooted territory.

suggestions.label: 💿 Gold (2023) — Cleo Sol
suggestions.note: Released just two weeks after Heaven — a companion piece and her most sonically lush album to date.

suggestions.label: 🤝 5 (2019) — SAULT
suggestions.note: SAULT's debut album with Cleo Sol on vocals — raw funk and soul that predates both Rose in the Dark and the collective's critical breakthrough.

suggestions.label: 📻 This is Cleo Sol
suggestions.note: Community-curated playlist spanning her solo work and features — a good overview if you want the highlights.

play_commands.title: Exploring: Sade
play_commands.narrative: You're hearing Sade through The Neptunes' lens — Pharrell Williams and Chad Hugo remixed 'By Your Side' for this 2011 compilation, bridging Sade's signature smooth soul with early-2000s beat science. The original track lives on Lovers Rock (2000), Sade's Grammy-winning fifth album that the band self-produced. What Spotify won't tell you: Sade's saxophonist Stuart Matthewman co-wrote and co-produced Maxwell's landmark Urban Hang Suite (1996), and the rest of the band formed Sweetback — a side project featuring Maxwell and Amel Larrieux that pushed Love Deluxe's electronic atmospherics even further. Their debut Diamond Life (1984) was produced by Robin Millar, who crafted that iconic warm-yet-precise sound.

suggestions.label: 💿 The Ultimate Collection (2011) — Sade
suggestions.note: The compilation this track is from — 29 tracks spanning their entire career, including rare Neptunes and Cottonbelly remixes.

suggestions.label: 💿 Lovers Rock (2000) — Sade
suggestions.note: The album 'By Your Side' originally appeared on — Grammy winner for Best Pop Vocal Album, self-produced by the band with dubwise rhythms and Matthewman's acoustic guitar.

suggestions.label: 🎛️ Maxwell's Urban Hang Suite (1996) — Maxwell
suggestions.note: Co-written and co-produced by Sade's Stuart Matthewman — if you love Sade's velvety arrangements, this is their DNA transplanted into neo-soul.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Sweetback (1996) — Sweetback
suggestions.note: Side project of Sade's Matthewman, Denman & Hale, featuring Maxwell and Amel Larrieux — a deeper, more electronic extension of Love Deluxe's sound.

suggestions.label: 💿 Love Deluxe (1992) — Sade
suggestions.note: Produced by the band with Mike Pela — features 'No Ordinary Love' and the trip-hop precursor 'Cherish the Day,' their most atmospheric album.

suggestions.label: 💿 Diamond Life (1984) — Sade
suggestions.note: Produced by Robin Millar — the debut that launched 'Smooth Operator,' went quadruple platinum, and defined sophisti-pop.

suggestions.label: 🎛️ In Search Of... (2002) — N.E.R.D
suggestions.note: The Neptunes' own band — Pharrell and Chad Hugo, who remixed the track you're listening to, channeling their production genius into genre-bending rock-funk.

suggestions.label: 📻 This Is Sade
suggestions.note: 54-track deep playlist spanning all six studio albums — a great way to explore beyond the hits.

play_commands.title: Exploring: Sigur Rós
play_commands.narrative: Hoppípolla pulled you into Sigur Rós — the Icelandic post-rock band built around Jónsi Birgisson's bowed guitar and falsetto vocals, active since 1994 out of Reykjavík. Takk... (2005) was self-produced by the band with engineer Ken Thomas, who also engineered their breakthrough Ágætis byrjun (1999) and the all-Hopelandic ( ) (2002). The production web fans out from there: Alex Somers (Jónsi's partner) co-produced their ambient masterpiece Valtari (2012) and collaborated with the band on Black Mirror's 'Hang the DJ' score, while their latest, ÁTTA (2023), was produced with Paul Corley — the same producer behind Ben Frost's and Oneohtrix Point Never's records. Jónsi's solo work and his ambient project with Somers (Riceboy Sleeps) extend the sonic world, and Amiina, the Icelandic string quartet who toured with Sigur Rós for years, released their own gorgeous debut Kurr in 2007.

suggestions.label: 💿 Takk... (2005) — Sigur Rós
suggestions.note: The album Hoppípolla comes from — self-produced by the band with engineer Ken Thomas at Sundlaugin Studio, their converted swimming pool in Mosfellsbær.

suggestions.label: 🎛️ ÁTTA (2023) — Sigur Rós
suggestions.note: Their comeback album, produced with Paul Corley — who also produced Ben Frost's Aurora and Oneohtrix Point Never's Garden of Delete. Same ethereal DNA, modern production.

suggestions.label: 💿 Ágætis byrjun (1999) — Sigur Rós
suggestions.note: The album that broke them internationally — engineered by Ken Thomas (same as Takk...). Praised by Radiohead, Bowie, and Coldplay; features 'Svefn-g-englar' used in Vanilla Sky.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Riceboy Sleeps (2009) — Jónsi & Alex Somers
suggestions.note: Jónsi's ambient collaboration with partner Alex Somers, who later co-produced Sigur Rós's Valtari. Pure texture and atmosphere — the quietest corner of the Sigur Rós universe.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Go (2010) — Jónsi
suggestions.note: Jónsi's exuberant solo debut — more pop-forward and percussion-heavy than Sigur Rós, with collaborations including Nico Muhly on arrangements. Same voice, completely different energy.

suggestions.label: 💿 Valtari (2012) — Sigur Rós
suggestions.note: Co-produced and mixed by Alex Somers with the band — their most ambient, introverted album. If Takk... is the sunrise, Valtari is the deep night.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Kurr (2007) — Amiina
suggestions.note: Debut album from Amiina, the Icelandic string quartet who toured with Sigur Rós for seven years and played on Takk... and ( ). Delicate, experimental chamber music.

suggestions.label: 📻 Sigur Rós — Complete Discography
suggestions.note: Official band-curated playlist spanning their entire catalog including the 20th Anniversary Takk... material — 283 tracks for deep immersion.

play_commands.title: Exploring: Boards of Canada
play_commands.narrative: Boards of Canada's Inferno is their first album in 13 years, breaking a silence that stretched back to 2013's Tomorrow's Harvest — and you're already in on it with 'Deep Time.' The Scottish brothers Mike Sandison and Marcus Eoin are famously reclusive and self-produced on their own Hexagon Sun label/studio, but their remix and production web runs deep: they've remixed Beck's 'Broken Drum' (from Guero/Guerolito), cLOUDDEAD's 'Dead Dogs Two,' Boom Bip's 'Last Walk Around Mirror Lake,' and multiple tracks for Nevermen (the Tunde Adebimpe / Doseone / Mike Patton supergroup). They've also produced for The Sexual Objects and are deeply embedded in the Warp Records ecosystem alongside Autechre, Aphex Twin, and Bibio. Their 2019 Societas x Tape NTS Radio mix is a rare window into their own influences.

suggestions.label: 💿 Inferno (2026) — Boards of Canada
suggestions.note: The album Deep Time is from — their long-awaited return after 13 years of silence, 18 tracks on Warp Records.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Guerolito (2005) — Beck
suggestions.note: Contains BoC's remix of Beck's Broken Drum — one of their most acclaimed remixes, transforming the track into hazy downtempo psychedelia.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Ten (2004) — cLOUDDEAD
suggestions.note: BoC remixed Dead Dogs Two from this album — cLOUDDEAD is Doseone and Why? from Anticon Records, abstract hip-hop meets ambient electronics.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Nevermen (2016) — Nevermen
suggestions.note: BoC remixed Mr Mistake and Treat Em Right for this supergroup of TV on the Radio's Tunde Adebimpe, Doseone, and Mike Patton (Faith No More).

suggestions.label: 🤝 Seed to Sun (2002) — Boom Bip
suggestions.note: BoC remixed Last Walk Around Mirror Lake from this Lex Records release — Boom Bip's glitchy instrumental hip-hop shares BoC's lo-fi tape aesthetic.

suggestions.label: 💿 Music Has the Right to Children (1998) — Boards of Canada
suggestions.note: Their landmark debut on Warp — the album that defined hauntological electronic music with its decayed samples of 1970s educational films and analog warmth.

suggestions.label: 💿 Geogaddi (2002) — Boards of Canada
suggestions.note: The darker, denser follow-up — obsessed with numerology and subliminal messages, 23 tracks totaling exactly 66 minutes and 6 seconds.

suggestions.label: 📻 Societas x Tape — Boards of Canada (NTS Radio, 2019)
suggestions.note: BoC's own 2-hour curated mix broadcast on NTS for Warp's 30th anniversary — a rare glimpse into the music that influences them (via bocpages.org).

play_commands.title: Exploring: Skye Newman
play_commands.narrative: Skye Newman is a 22-year-old South London singer-songwriter from SE9 who just became the first UK female solo artist to chart Top 20 with her debut single and follow-up since Ella Henderson in 2014 — signed to Columbia/Sony Music UK. "Woman I Am" is co-written with her long-time production team Boo (Benjy Gibson) and Luis Navidad, who were previously in a band called Bamily together before becoming her dedicated producers. The full SE9 album also features production from Maverick Sabre, Al Shux (known for Jay-Z and Alicia Keys work), Chrissi, Ed Thomas, and Jonah Stevens — a deep bench of collaborators for a debut project. Her sound blends rap storytelling with soul depth, influenced by Amy Winehouse, Adele, and Eminem.

suggestions.label: Woman I Am · Skye Newman
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: 💿 SE9 (2026) — Skye Newman
suggestions.note: The full 14-track debut album Woman I Am lives on — produced by Boo & Luis Navidad with contributions from Maverick Sabre, Al Shux, and Chrissi.

suggestions.label: 🎛️ Don't Forget to Look Up (2022) — Maverick Sabre
suggestions.note: Maverick Sabre produced tracks on SE9 — his own album shares that same raw, confessional London soul energy. Irish-London singer-songwriter with a devoted following.

suggestions.label: 🎛️ Burn The Right Things Down (2024) — Maverick Sabre
suggestions.note: Maverick Sabre's most recent album before working on SE9 — 16 tracks of emotionally heavy soul-pop from another artist who blends spoken-word honesty with melody.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Back In The Day (2021) — Chrissi
suggestions.note: Chrissi co-wrote Walk on SE9 — her first collaboration with Skye. Chrissi is a UK songwriter/artist in her own right with a similar raw, intimate style.

suggestions.label: 💿 SE9 Part 1 (2025) — Skye Newman
suggestions.note: Skye's 6-track debut EP that started the breakout — includes Hairdresser (Top 30) and Family Matters (Top 8), both produced by Boo & Luis Navidad.

suggestions.label: 🎵 Chapter One (2014) — Ella Henderson
suggestions.note: Skye is the first UK female solo artist since Ella Henderson to debut Top 20 with her first two singles — Chapter One features Ghost, which set that benchmark.

suggestions.label: 📻 This is Skye Newman
suggestions.note: Fan-curated comprehensive playlist covering all her singles and EP tracks — good overview of her full catalogue so far.

play_commands.title: Exploring: Oliver Nelson
play_commands.album: The Blues And The Abstract Truth
play_commands.narrative: You landed on one of the most perfectly assembled sessions in jazz history. "Stolen Moments" is a certified jazz standard, and the whole album was recorded in a single February 1961 session at Van Gelder Studio — produced by Creed Taylor for Impulse! Records. The personnel is staggering: your most-played artist Bill Evans (39 plays) is on piano — this was his ONLY session with Nelson — alongside Freddie Hubbard on trumpet, Eric Dolphy on alto sax and flute, Paul Chambers on bass, and Roy Haynes on drums. Nelson was as much an arranger as a player — he later arranged Sonny Rollins' Alfie soundtrack and scored extensively for TV and film. Steely Dan (also in your library) used "Teenie's Blues" from this album as a 2009 concert opener. The album was just added to the Library of Congress National Recording Registry in 2026.

suggestions.label: Stolen Moments · Oliver Nelson
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: The Blues And The Abstract Truth (1961) — Oliver Nelson
suggestions.note: The album this track is from — play the whole thing. Produced by Creed Taylor, engineered by Rudy Van Gelder, featuring Bill Evans on his only session with Nelson.

suggestions.label: Straight Ahead (1961) — Oliver Nelson & Eric Dolphy
suggestions.note: Recorded the same year as Abstract Truth, again featuring Eric Dolphy. Nelson and Dolphy had a deep Prestige Records partnership before this.

suggestions.label: More Blues And The Abstract Truth (1964) — Oliver Nelson
suggestions.note: Nelson's sequel with an entirely different (larger) ensemble — a fascinating contrast to the tight sextet of the original.

suggestions.label: Sound Pieces (1966) — Oliver Nelson
suggestions.note: Nelson moving further into orchestral arranging — the bridge between his small-group work and his later TV/film scoring career.

suggestions.label: Out To Lunch! (1964) — Eric Dolphy
suggestions.note: Dolphy's masterpiece, recorded months before his death at 36. Freddie Hubbard plays trumpet here too — the same frontline partnership as Abstract Truth.

suggestions.label: Interplay (1962) — Bill Evans
suggestions.note: Evans and Hubbard together again, just a year after Abstract Truth — a rare quintet date for Evans with Hubbard on trumpet and Jim Hall on guitar.

suggestions.label: Red Clay (1970) — Freddie Hubbard
suggestions.note: Hubbard's own classic as a leader — the hard bop-to-fusion pivot album. Also produced by Creed Taylor (same producer as Abstract Truth) at CTI Records.

suggestions.label: Fantabulous (1964) — Oliver Nelson
suggestions.note: A lesser-known Nelson big band album — shows his evolution from small-group player to large ensemble arranger.

play_commands.title: Exploring: Neil Young
play_commands.narrative: You've played Harvest Moon 6 times — it's clearly a favorite. That album was co-produced by Neil Young and Ben Keith, his long-time pedal steel player who also played on the original Harvest in 1972. Most of Young's classic '70s work was produced by David Briggs, his most important creative partner from 1969 until Briggs' death in 1995 — while his 2010 album Le Noise took a radical left turn under Daniel Lanois (who also produced U2's The Joshua Tree and Bob Dylan's Time Out of Mind). Beyond his solo work, Young's collaboration web runs deep: Crosby, Stills, Nash & Young's Déjà Vu, Mirror Ball recorded live with Pearl Jam as his backing band, and decades of raw electric albums with Crazy Horse.

suggestions.label: Harvest Moon · Neil Young
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: 💿 Harvest Moon (1992) — Neil Young
suggestions.note: The album this track is from — play the whole thing. Co-produced by Neil Young and Ben Keith, it's the warm acoustic sequel to Harvest recorded 20 years later with many of the same musicians.

suggestions.label: 🎛️ Harvest (1972) — Neil Young
suggestions.note: The original companion to Harvest Moon, co-produced by Elliot Mazer and Neil Young. Features the London Symphony Orchestra on 'A Man Needs a Maid' and 'There's a World' — same producer/musician circle as Harvest Moon.

suggestions.label: 🎛️ Le Noise (2010) — Neil Young
suggestions.note: Produced by Daniel Lanois (U2's The Joshua Tree, Bob Dylan's Time Out of Mind) — a completely solo, mostly electric album that strips Neil down to just voice and guitar run through Lanois' signature ambient processing.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Déjà Vu (1970) — Crosby, Stills, Nash & Young
suggestions.note: Young's most famous collaboration — he joined CSN as fourth member and contributed 'Helpless' and 'Country Girl.' Produced by the band with Dallas Taylor and Greg Reeves.

suggestions.label: 🤝 Mirror Ball (1995) — Neil Young
suggestions.note: Neil Young backed by Pearl Jam — a trans-generational guitar collaboration recorded live in the studio. Brendan O'Brien produced. Eddie Vedder and company bring raw grunge energy to Young's songwriting.

suggestions.label: 💿 After the Gold Rush (1970) — Neil Young
suggestions.note: Produced by Neil Young and David Briggs — his most important producer who shaped the raw, intimate sound from 1969 to 1995. Features a young Nils Lofgren on guitar and piano.

suggestions.label: 💿 Everybody Knows This Is Nowhere (1969) — Neil Young & Crazy Horse
suggestions.note: The first Neil Young & Crazy Horse album, produced by David Briggs. Features the epic guitar workouts 'Down by the River' and 'Cowgirl in the Sand' — the template for their decades-long partnership.

suggestions.label: 💿 On the Beach (1974) — Neil Young
suggestions.note: You've already dipped into this one — a dark, brooding masterpiece from Young's 'Ditch Trilogy.' Produced by Neil Young, David Briggs, and Mark Harman with contributions from The Band's Levon Helm and Rick Danko.

suggestions.label: 📻 This is Neil Young
suggestions.note: A community-curated playlist spanning Neil's entire career — 91 tracks from Buffalo Springfield through the Chrome Hearts era.

play_commands.title: Exploring: The Cult
play_commands.narrative: Your first Cult track lands right at the heart of their best record. Love (1985) was produced by Steve Brown, the same producer behind Wham Make It Big, who transformed Ian Astbury and Billy Duffy post-punk roots into shimmering psychedelic rock. The real twist came next: unhappy with Brown sound for the follow-up, they flew to New York and Rick Rubin re-recorded the entire album as Electric (1987), stripping it to raw AC/DC-style hard rock. Bob Rock (who later did Metallica Black Album) then produced Sonic Temple (1989) and three more Cult records. A hidden thread: current bassist Charlie Jones played in both Robert Plant band and Peter Gabriel touring group, connecting The Cult directly to your Gabriel listening.

suggestions.label: Rain · The Cult
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: Love (1985) - The Cult
suggestions.note: The album Rain comes from. Steve Brown shimmering production made She Sells Sanctuary and Rain into UK hits.

suggestions.label: Electric (1987) - The Cult
suggestions.note: Rick Rubin scrapped and re-recorded the entire Love follow-up, turning The Cult into a hard rock band overnight.

suggestions.label: Sonic Temple (1989) - The Cult
suggestions.note: Bob Rock first Cult production. Fire Woman hit Top 10 in the US and UK.

suggestions.label: Dreamtime (1984) - The Cult
suggestions.note: The debut, more gothic post-punk. Spiritwalker hit No.1 on the UK Indie Chart. Billy Duffy had just left Theatre of Hate.

suggestions.label: The Cult Radio (132 tracks)
suggestions.note: Community-curated deep playlist spanning the full catalog.

suggestions.label: Under the Midnight Sun (2022) - The Cult
suggestions.note: Their most recent album. Astbury and Duffy still going 40 years on.

suggestions.label: Ceremony (1991) - The Cult
suggestions.note: The tension album. Matt Sorum on tour drums before joining Guns N Roses.

suggestions.label: Hidden City (2016) - The Cult
suggestions.note: Bob Rock returns to produce. Darker, more cinematic than their 80s work.

play_commands.title: Exploring: Cécile McLorin Salvant
play_commands.narrative: Cécile McLorin Salvant has 4 tracks in your library but hasn't appeared in your recent listening sessions — this is an artist worth going deeper on. A three-time Grammy winner for Best Jazz Vocal Album, Salvant's creative arc runs from straight-ahead jazz with pianist Aaron Diehl (WomanChild, For One to Love) through increasingly adventurous territory: covering Kate Bush and Sting on Ghost Song, a French/Creole folk-myth concept on Mélusine, and a self-produced electronic-jazz hybrid on Oh Snap. Her most important musical partnership is with pianist Sullivan Fortner, who appears across Dreams and Daggers, The Window, and Oh Snap — and she's also a member of ARTEMIS, the Blue Note all-female jazz supergroup alongside Renee Rosnes, Ingrid Jensen, and Melissa Aldana.

suggestions.label: The Window (2018) — Cécile McLorin Salvant
suggestions.note: Intimate duo session with Sullivan Fortner — won her third consecutive Grammy for Best Jazz Vocal Album. Covers Stevie Wonder, Dori Caymmi, and Norma Winstone alongside standards.

suggestions.label: Ghost Song (2022) — Cécile McLorin Salvant
suggestions.note: Her most genre-crossing album: reinterprets Kate Bush's Wuthering Heights and Sting's Until alongside haunting originals.

suggestions.label: Oh Snap (2025) — Cécile McLorin Salvant
suggestions.note: Self-produced with Jack DeBoe — a radical departure mixing electronic pop, folk, and jazz, built from childhood memories of 1990s Miami.

suggestions.label: ARTEMIS (2020) — ARTEMIS
suggestions.note: Blue Note supergroup featuring Salvant alongside pianist Renee Rosnes, trumpeter Ingrid Jensen, saxophonist Melissa Aldana.

suggestions.label: Southern Nights (2025) — Sullivan Fortner
suggestions.note: Salvant's most important musical partner — Fortner is the pianist on The Window, Dreams and Daggers, and Oh Snap.

suggestions.label: Linger Awhile (2022) — Samara Joy
suggestions.note: The most direct heir to Salvant's straight-ahead vocal jazz legacy — Joy won the Grammy for Best New Artist in 2023.

suggestions.label: Dreams and Daggers (2017) — Cécile McLorin Salvant
suggestions.note: Double-disc live album recorded at the Village Vanguard — features both the Aaron Diehl trio and Sullivan Fortner plus the Catalyst Quartet.

suggestions.label: Mélusine (2023) — Cécile McLorin Salvant
suggestions.note: Concept album inspired by the French folk myth of a water spirit. Sung in French, Haitian Creole, and Occitan.

play_commands.title: Exploring: Thee Sacred Souls
play_commands.narrative: Thee Sacred Souls are a San Diego sweet soul trio — Josh Lane, Sal Samano, and Alex Garcia — discovered by Daptone co-founder Gabriel Roth (aka Bosco Mann) after just their second live show. Both albums, the self-titled debut (2022) and Got a Story to Tell (2024), were produced by Bosco Mann at Penrose Studios in Riverside, CA, placing them in the direct lineage of Sharon Jones & The Dap-Kings and Charles Bradley — all shaped by the same producer's analog, vintage-soul aesthetic. Their sound draws on chicano soul, doo-wop, and gospel oldies, and they've toured alongside St. Paul and The Broken Bones and Nathaniel Rateliff & the Night Sweats.

suggestions.label: Live For You · Thee Sacred Souls
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: Got a Story to Tell (2024) — Thee Sacred Souls
suggestions.note: Their sophomore album, also produced by Bosco Mann at Penrose Studios — deeper grooves and more confident songwriting from the same team that made the debut.

suggestions.label: 100 Days, 100 Nights (2007) — Sharon Jones & The Dap-Kings
suggestions.note: Same producer: Bosco Mann wrote, arranged, and produced this — the Daptone blueprint that Thee Sacred Souls inherited. Peak Sharon Jones.

suggestions.label: No Time For Dreaming (2011) — Charles Bradley
suggestions.note: Another Bosco Mann/Daptone production — raw, emotional deep soul from the Screaming Eagle of Soul, recorded in the same analog tradition.

suggestions.label: Sinseerly Yours (2024) — Thee Sinseers
suggestions.note: Fellow SoCal chicano soul revivalists led by Joey Quiñones — they share the same oldies/doo-wop DNA and come from the same San Diego-to-LA scene.

suggestions.label: Come Around and Love Me (2023) — Jalen Ngonda
suggestions.note: Also on Daptone Records, produced by Bosco Mann — sweet soul from a newer Daptone signee with a Smokey Robinson-esque falsetto.

suggestions.label: I Learned the Hard Way (2010) — Sharon Jones & The Dap-Kings
suggestions.note: Bosco Mann production, more polished and Motown-influenced than earlier Dap-Kings records — the album that broke them into the mainstream.

suggestions.label: This is Thee Sacred Souls (playlist)
suggestions.note: Community-curated playlist of the full Thee Sacred Souls catalog with some adjacent picks — good for extended listening.

play_commands.title: Exploring: Durand Jones & The Indications
play_commands.narrative: You're just getting into Durand Jones & The Indications — 'Flower Moon' is your entry point from their upcoming Flowers album. This Bloomington, Indiana soul trio (Jones, drummer/vocalist Aaron Frazer, guitarist Blake Rhein) self-produces all their records and started by recording on a four-track Tascam in 2012. The key connection to follow is Aaron Frazer's solo debut, produced by Dan Auerbach of The Black Keys at his Easy Eye Sound studio — same producer behind records for Yola, Robert Finley, and The Pretenders. Guitarist Blake Rhein moonlights as a researcher for The Numero Group reissue label, and Private Space features harpist Brandee Younger and contributions from Eli 'Paperboy' Reed.

suggestions.label: Sea Gets Hotter · Durand Jones & The Indications
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: Introducing... (2021) — Aaron Frazer
suggestions.note: Solo debut from the Indications' drummer/co-vocalist, produced by Dan Auerbach (Black Keys) at Easy Eye Sound — silky falsetto soul with vintage Nashville production

suggestions.label: American Love Call (2019) — Durand Jones & The Indications
suggestions.note: Their vocal group harmonies album — self-produced in Brooklyn, this is where Jones and Frazer's dual-lead approach really crystallized

suggestions.label: Private Space (2021) — Durand Jones & The Indications
suggestions.note: Their disco/modern soul pivot, recorded at Diamond Mine NYC — features harpist Brandee Younger and Eli 'Paperboy' Reed, self-produced by the band

suggestions.label: Wait Til I Get Over (2023) — Durand Jones
suggestions.note: Jones' solo debut chronicling his hometown of Hillaryville, Louisiana — a deeply personal album blending soul, gospel, and country that NPR featured for a Tiny Desk Concert

suggestions.label: Into The Blue (2024) — Aaron Frazer
suggestions.note: Frazer's second solo album — building on the Dan Auerbach connection from Introducing..., deeper into blue-eyed soul territory

suggestions.label: Thee Sacred Souls (2022) — Thee Sacred Souls
suggestions.note: Fellow soul revivalists from San Diego on Daptone Records — same lowrider community fan base that championed DJ&TI, co-headlining festivals together

suggestions.label: Durand Jones & The Indications (2016) — Durand Jones & The Indications
suggestions.note: Where it all started — recorded on a four-track Tascam, originally on Colemine Records, raw lo-fi soul that caught the indie record store underground

suggestions.label: This Is Durand Jones & The Indications
suggestions.note: Community-curated playlist spanning all four albums plus singles — good overview if you want the highlights before diving into full albums

play_commands.title: Exploring: Moses Boyd
play_commands.narrative: Moses Boyd is the drummer-producer at the nerve center of London's 21st-century jazz explosion — self-producing his Mercury-nominated Dark Matter (2020) while drumming on landmark albums by nearly every key figure in the scene. The production thread here is wild: Boyd produced Zara McFarlane's Arise, co-produced Theon Cross's Fyah, drummed across both Joe Armon-Jones albums, played on Sons of Kemet's Your Queen Is a Reptile, and even produced Beyoncé's "My Power" on The Lion King: The Gift. His duo with saxophonist Binker Golding (Binker & Moses) represents the raw, semi-free side of his playing — four albums of pure sax-and-drums improvisation — while his latest collaboration with pianist Greg Foat on Opening Time (2025) shows a gentler, more cinematic register. The dual influence he cites — Miles Davis's Kind of Blue and Dizzee Rascal's Boy in da Corner — explains everything about how Dark Matter fuses broken beat, grime, and highlife into jazz without pretension.

suggestions.label: Stranger Than Fiction · Moses Boyd
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: Arise (2017) — Zara McFarlane
suggestions.note: Boyd PRODUCED this entire album — his most significant production credit outside his own work. McFarlane's vocals over Boyd's rhythmic frameworks.

suggestions.label: Journey to the Mountain of Forever (2017) — Binker and Moses
suggestions.note: The most expansive Binker & Moses album — 15 tracks of raw sax-and-drums improvisation, the semi-free side Dark Matter only hints at.

suggestions.label: Feeding the Machine (2022) — Binker and Moses
suggestions.note: Their fourth album, co-produced by Grammy-winner Hugh Padgham — wholly improvised but with a tighter, more engineered sound than earlier records.

suggestions.label: Displaced Diaspora (2018) — Moses Boyd
suggestions.note: Boyd's pre-Dark Matter statement with the Exodus band — Theon Cross on tuba, Binker Golding on sax, more live-band energy and less electronic processing.

suggestions.label: SOURCE (2020) — Nubya Garcia
suggestions.note: Garcia's debut — Boyd drummed on her earlier Nubya's 5ive EP. Same Tomorrow's Warriors pipeline, same South London scene, but her own spiritual jazz voice.

suggestions.label: Your Queen Is a Reptile (2018) — Sons of Kemet
suggestions.note: Shabaka Hutchings' fiery ensemble — Boyd drums on three tracks. The political counterpart to Dark Matter's dancefloor energy.

suggestions.label: Opening Time (2025) — Greg Foat, Jihad Darwish & Moses Boyd
suggestions.note: Boyd's latest — a cinematic piano-drums-bass trio with Greg Foat, their third collaboration after The Mage (2019) and Symphonie Pacifique (2020).

play_commands.title: Exploring: Black Pumas
play_commands.narrative: Black Pumas is the Austin-born psychedelic soul duo of singer Eric Burton (a former Santa Monica busker and American Idol contestant) and guitarist/producer Adrian Quesada, whose production fingerprints stretch across an incredible web of Latin funk, psych-soul, and Chicano R&B. Quesada won a Grammy with Grupo Fantasma for El Existential (2010), runs Electric Deluxe Recorders in Austin where he's produced over 150 records, and fronts the Latin funk-rock outfit Brownout — whose Brown Sabbath project reimagines Black Sabbath through a cumbia-funk lens. Both Black Pumas albums were produced by Quesada at Electric Deluxe, with Chronicles of a Diamond adding co-production from John Congleton (St. Vincent, Angel Olsen). Quesada's solo work reveals his full range: Boleros Psicodélicos reinvents classic Latin boleros with psychedelic production, Look at My Soul maps Texas Chicano soul, and Jaguar Sound explores Afro-Latin funk.

suggestions.label: Black Moon Rising · Black Pumas
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: Boleros Psicodélicos (2022) — Adrian Quesada
suggestions.note: Quesada's Grammy-nominated solo album reinventing classic Latin boleros with the same psychedelic production ear he brings to Black Pumas — featuring iLe, Girl Ultra, and Money Mark

suggestions.label: Chronicles of a Diamond (2023) — Black Pumas
suggestions.note: Their sophomore album co-produced by John Congleton (St. Vincent, Angel Olsen) alongside Quesada — a richer, more cinematic evolution from the debut

suggestions.label: Look at My Soul: The Latin Shade of Texas Soul (2018) — Adrian Quesada
suggestions.note: Quesada's pre-Black Pumas compilation mapping the hidden Chicano soul of Texas — the direct precursor to the sound he'd refine with Eric Burton

suggestions.label: El Existential (2010) — Grupo Fantasma
suggestions.note: The Grammy-winning Latin funk album from Quesada's other band Grupo Fantasma — 12-piece Austin powerhouse blending cumbia, funk, and salsa

suggestions.label: Brownout Presents Brown Sabbath (2014) — Brownout
suggestions.note: Quesada's Brownout reimagines Black Sabbath riffs through Latin funk and cumbia — same heavy guitar instincts, completely different cultural lens

suggestions.label: Jaguar Sound (2022) — Adrian Quesada
suggestions.note: Quesada's Afro-Latin funk instrumental album — if you love Black Pumas' grooves, this is the producer unfiltered

suggestions.label: Black Pumas (2019) — Black Pumas
suggestions.note: The debut that earned a Best New Artist Grammy nom and Album of the Year nom — Colors alone went Platinum, recorded at Quesada's Electric Deluxe studio

suggestions.label: This Is Black Pumas
suggestions.note: Comprehensive playlist spanning both albums, deluxe tracks, and live recordings — good entry point for the full catalog

play_commands.title: Exploring: Kate & Anna McGarrigle
play_commands.narrative: "Better Times Are Coming" pulls from the Civil War folk tradition that Kate & Anna McGarrigle threaded through their entire career — they first recorded it for the 1991 compilation Songs of the Civil War alongside a teenage Rufus Wainwright. Their producer Joe Boyd (who built the sound of Nick Drake and Fairport Convention) shaped their 1975 debut's intimate architecture, while their collaboration web stretches from Mark Knopfler guesting on Love Over and Over to providing backing vocals on Nick Cave's No More Shall We Part at Abbey Road — and Linda Ronstadt's platinum cover of Anna's "Heart Like a Wheel" is what launched their recording career in the first place.

suggestions.label: Log Driver's Waltz · Kate & Anna McGarrigle
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: Kate & Anna McGarrigle (1975) — Kate & Anna McGarrigle
suggestions.note: Their Joe Boyd-produced debut — Melody Maker's Album of the Year in 1976, featuring Heart Like a Wheel and Mendocino

suggestions.label: Heart Like a Wheel (1974) — Linda Ronstadt
suggestions.note: The platinum album that broke the McGarrigles — the title track is Anna McGarrigle's composition, which got them signed to Warner Bros.

suggestions.label: Five Leaves Left (1969) — Nick Drake
suggestions.note: Produced by Joe Boyd, same as the McGarrigles' debut — Boyd's gift for capturing intimate folk vocals connects these two directly

suggestions.label: Liege & Lief (1969) — Fairport Convention
suggestions.note: Another Joe Boyd production — the album that invented British folk-rock, blending traditional songs with electric arrangements just as the McGarrigles blended French-Canadian and Anglo folk

suggestions.label: Folkocracy (2023) — Rufus Wainwright
suggestions.note: Kate's son's folk covers album — Anna, Martha Wainwright, and Lily Lanken join on Wild Mountain Thyme, carrying the McGarrigle family tradition forward

suggestions.label: The McGarrigle Hour (1998) — Kate & Anna McGarrigle
suggestions.note: The family gathering album — Emmylou Harris, Rufus and Martha Wainwright, and sister Jane all appear on this Juno Award-winning collection

suggestions.label: Matapédia (1996) — Kate & Anna McGarrigle
suggestions.note: Their Juno Award winner for Roots Album of the Year — nostalgic songs inspired by Quebec's Matapédia Valley, their most acclaimed late-career work

suggestions.label: Kate & Anna McGarrigle ESSENTIALS
suggestions.note: 30-track curated playlist spanning their career — a good overview if you want to explore beyond Odditties

play_commands.title: Exploring: João Donato
play_commands.narrative: You're listening to "Aquarius (Bring Her Back Home)" from the Joyce Moreno & João Donato collaboration — a joyful 2010 session on Far Out Recordings where two Brazilian legends traded ideas in the studio. Donato (1934–2023) was a foundational figure in bossa nova: his 1956 debut was produced by Antonio Carlos Jobim himself, and João Gilberto once credited Donato as an inspiration for bossa nova's creation. His reach extends far beyond Brazil — he played trombone with Eddie Palmieri's La Perfecta and Tito Puente's big band in 1960s New York, recorded A Bad Donato (1970) with Ron Carter on bass, served as music director for Gal Costa, and appeared on albums by Bebel Gilberto, Caetano Veloso, Marisa Monte, and Milton Nascimento. His 2021 Jazz Is Dead session (JID007) with Adrian Younge and Ali Shaheed Muhammad brought his Cuban-inflected bossa to a whole new audience.

suggestions.label: Aquarius (Bring Her Back Home) · João Donato
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: A Bad Donato (1970) — João Donato
suggestions.note: 🎛️ Recorded in the US with Ron Carter on bass — Donato's jazz-fusion masterpiece that merged bossa nova, funk, and Latin rhythms on Blue Thumb Records

suggestions.label: Aquarius (2010) — Joyce Moreno feat. João Donato
suggestions.note: 🤝 The full album you're hearing — Joyce and Donato co-wrote six of its thirteen tracks, including 'Aquarius' and 'No Fundo do Mar'

suggestions.label: Donato Deodato (1973) — João Donato
suggestions.note: 🎛️ A meeting of two Brazilian arranging giants — Donato and Eumir Deodato trading compositions on Muse Records

suggestions.label: Quem É Quem (1973) — João Donato
suggestions.note: 💿 One of Donato's most beloved albums, produced by Marcos Valle — features 'Até Quem Sabe' and the signature sambolero grooves

suggestions.label: Previsão do Tempo (1973) — Marcos Valle
suggestions.note: 🎛️ Marcos Valle produced Donato's Quem É Quem — this is Valle's own psychedelic-bossa masterpiece from the same year

suggestions.label: João Donato JID007 (2021) — João Donato, Adrian Younge & Ali Shaheed Muhammad
suggestions.note: 🤝 Donato's final major recording — a Jazz Is Dead session with A Tribe Called Quest's Ali Shaheed Muhammad and producer Adrian Younge

suggestions.label: Prelude (1972) — Deodato
suggestions.note: 🎛️ Eumir Deodato's breakout CTI Records album — the Brazilian arranger who shared a whole album with Donato on Donato Deodato

suggestions.label: Muito À Vontade (1962) — João Donato
suggestions.note: 💿 Early Donato — recorded during the golden age of bossa nova, before he left for New York to play with Mongo Santamaría and Eddie Palmieri

play_commands.title: Exploring: Electric Guest
play_commands.narrative: You've been spinning Electric Guest's Mondo — the Danger Mouse-produced debut that Rolling Stone called "a Beck-ian journey into L.A. slacker soul." Asa Taccone literally moved into Danger Mouse's old room in a house full of musicians, where he met drummer Matthew "Cornbread" Compton; Burton heard their demos and spent a year coaxing Mondo into existence. That Danger Mouse connection opens a deep production web: he also produced Broken Bells (with James Mercer of The Shins), Gnarls Barkley (with CeeLo Green), and recently Cheat Codes with Black Thought. Meanwhile, Taccone has become a sought-after songwriter/producer himself — co-writing with Carly Rae Jepsen ("Feels Right" on Dedicated Side B), collaborating with Portugal. The Man, and even working with The Weeknd. Electric Guest's later albums — Plural, Kin, and the brand-new 10K — are all self-produced by Taccone, showing his evolution from Danger Mouse protégé to auteur.

suggestions.label: Amber · Electric Guest
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: Broken Bells (2010) — Broken Bells
suggestions.note: Danger Mouse + James Mercer (The Shins) — same producer as Mondo, with that same dreamy, layered pop sensibility

suggestions.label: St. Elsewhere (2006) — Gnarls Barkley
suggestions.note: Danger Mouse + CeeLo Green — the soulful, genre-bending side of Burton's production that directly influenced Mondo's retro grooves

suggestions.label: Plural (2017) — Electric Guest
suggestions.note: Taccone's first fully self-produced album after the Danger Mouse era — darker, more electronic, with standout single Oh Devil

suggestions.label: KIN (2019) — Electric Guest
suggestions.note: Features collaborations with Devin Di Dakta, Wafia, and Soleima — Taccone expanding into global pop territory

suggestions.label: 10K (2025) — Electric Guest
suggestions.note: Their first album in six years — a personal, triumphant return self-produced by Taccone and Compton

suggestions.label: Dedicated Side B (2020) — Carly Rae Jepsen
suggestions.note: Features Feels Right, co-written by Asa Taccone and Matt Compton — the Electric Guest pop sensibility filtered through CRJ

suggestions.label: Woodstock (2017) — Portugal. The Man
suggestions.note: Asa Taccone has written for and collaborated with Portugal. The Man — fellow indie-pop explorers with crossover ambitions

suggestions.label: Cheat Codes (2022) — Danger Mouse & Black Thought
suggestions.note: Danger Mouse's most recent production masterclass — hip-hop meets cinematic soul, showing where Burton went after Mondo

play_commands.title: Exploring: Broken Bells
play_commands.narrative: Broken Bells is Danger Mouse (Brian Burton) and James Mercer of The Shins — and "Mongrel Heart" is a fascinating thread-pull: Wikipedia specifically notes its cinematic quality as a precursor to Danger Mouse's Rome album with Daniele Luppi, featuring Jack White and Norah Jones. Burton is one of the most prolific producers of the 21st century, having shaped records for The Black Keys, Gorillaz, Beck, Adele, Parquet Courts, and the Red Hot Chili Peppers. With 9 cached tracks all from the self-titled debut, there are two more Broken Bells albums to discover, plus a vast web of Danger Mouse productions that share the same dreamy, layered aesthetic.

suggestions.label: Mongrel Heart · Broken Bells
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: After the Disco (2014) — Broken Bells
suggestions.note: Broken Bells' second album pushes into retro-futuristic synth-pop territory — same Danger Mouse + Mercer magic, bigger production scope

suggestions.label: INTO THE BLUE (2022) — Broken Bells
suggestions.note: Their long-awaited third album after an 8-year hiatus — more atmospheric and introspective, Burton and Mercer at their most refined

suggestions.label: Rome (2011) — Danger Mouse & Daniele Luppi
suggestions.note: Wikipedia cites Mongrel Heart as sharing DNA with this Spaghetti Western–inspired album featuring Jack White and Norah Jones on vocals

suggestions.label: Dark Night of the Soul (2009) — Danger Mouse & Sparklehorse
suggestions.note: Danger Mouse's haunting collaboration with Mark Linkous featuring guests like The Flaming Lips, Gruff Rhys, Julian Casablancas, and David Lynch on visuals

suggestions.label: St. Elsewhere (2006) — Gnarls Barkley
suggestions.note: Danger Mouse's other great vocal partnership — with CeeLo Green. Same layered production DNA, shifted into soul/hip-hop. Home of 'Crazy'

suggestions.label: Mondo (2012) — Electric Guest
suggestions.note: Entirely produced by Danger Mouse — dreamy indie-pop with the same warm, textured feel as Broken Bells. Burton's fingerprints are all over this

suggestions.label: Chutes Too Narrow (2003) — The Shins
suggestions.note: James Mercer's songwriting peak with The Shins before forming Broken Bells — the jangly melodies that drew Danger Mouse to collaborate

suggestions.label: Lux Prima (2019) — Karen O & Danger Mouse
suggestions.note: Danger Mouse pairs with Yeah Yeah Yeahs' Karen O for spacious, psychedelic art-pop — another window into his producer-as-auteur approach

play_commands.title: Exploring: David Lynch
play_commands.narrative: "So Glad" lives on Crazy Clown Time, Lynch's 2011 debut solo album — an "electronic blues" record he composed and performed with longtime sound engineer Dean Hurley, featuring Karen O of the Yeah Yeah Yeahs on "Pinky's Dream." Lynch's musical world extends far beyond his own vocals: he co-wrote lyrics and produced Julee Cruise's iconic Floating into the Night (1989) and The Voice of Love (1993) with Angelo Badalamenti, the composer behind virtually every Lynch film score from Blue Velvet onward. Lynch and Badalamenti also recorded Thought Gang around 1993 — a free-jazz experiment not released until 2018 — and Lynch guested on Flying Lotus's Flamagra (2019), provided vocals for Danger Mouse and Sparklehorse's Dark Night of the Soul (2010), produced Donovan's "I Am the Shaman" (2021), remixed Duran Duran, Moby, Zola Jesus, and Agnes Obel, and made two albums with vocalist Chrystabell including his final release, Cellophane Memories (2024). The Lost Highway soundtrack was produced by Trent Reznor — a rare case of Lynch ceding sonic control to another auteur.

suggestions.label: Pinky's Dream · David Lynch
suggestions.note: The track that started this exploration

suggestions.label: The Big Dream (2013) — David Lynch
suggestions.note: Lynch's second solo album, also with Dean Hurley — features Lykke Li on lead single "I'm Waiting Here" and goes deeper into dreamy electronic blues

suggestions.label: Floating into the Night (1989) — Julee Cruise
suggestions.note: Produced and co-written by Lynch with Badalamenti — the ethereal dream pop that soundtracked Twin Peaks, including "Falling" and "Mysteries of Love"

suggestions.label: Thought Gang (2018) — David Lynch & Angelo Badalamenti
suggestions.note: Lynch and Badalamenti's lost free-jazz experiment, recorded ~1993 but shelved for 25 years — tracks appeared in Fire Walk with Me and The Return

suggestions.label: Dark Night of the Soul (2010) — Danger Mouse & Sparklehorse
suggestions.note: Lynch provides guest vocals and contributed photographs for the art book — a haunting collaboration from Sparklehorse's final sessions before Mark Linkous's death

suggestions.label: Cellophane Memories (2024) — Chrystabell & David Lynch
suggestions.note: Lynch's final album release — a collaboration with longtime musical partner Chrystabell, continuing the ambient dream pop they began on This Train (2011)

suggestions.label: Flamagra (2019) — Flying Lotus
suggestions.note: Lynch co-wrote and provides spoken-word vocals on "Fire is Coming" — a surreal meeting of FlyLo's electronic experimentalism with Lynchian atmosphere

suggestions.label: Eraserhead Soundtrack (1982) — David Lynch & Alan R. Splet
suggestions.note: Where Lynch's sound design obsession began — industrial ambient drones co-created with Alan Splet for his 1977 debut film, including "In Heaven (Lady in the Radiator Song)"

suggestions.label: This is David Lynch
suggestions.note: Sacred Bones Records' curated overview of Lynch's musical output — good starting point for the full breadth of his solo and collaborative work

play_commands.title: Exploring: Karen O
play_commands.narrative: Karen O is one of rock's great collaboration magnets — the Yeah Yeah Yeahs frontwoman whose work orbits around producer Dave Sitek of TV on the Radio, who shaped their sound from Fever to Tell through Cool It Down. Outside YYY, she's built a remarkable web of one-off partnerships: a full album with Danger Mouse (Lux Prima), vocals on Flaming Lips' experimental Embryonic, a haunting turn on Swans' epic The Seer, David Lynch's debut album Crazy Clown Time, and an Immigrant Song cover with Trent Reznor and Atticus Ross for The Girl with the Dragon Tattoo. The Danger Mouse connection alone opens a door to Broken Bells and The Black Keys — all worth following from here.

suggestions.label: Lux Prima (2019) — Karen O & Danger Mouse
suggestions.note: Karen O's lush, cinematic collaboration with Danger Mouse — 11 years in the making, nominated for Grammy for Best Rock Performance

suggestions.label: Fever to Tell (2003) — Yeah Yeah Yeahs
suggestions.note: The debut that launched Karen O into the NYC post-punk revival — produced by Dave Sitek of TV on the Radio, featuring the iconic 'Maps'

suggestions.label: Return to Cookie Mountain (2006) — TV on the Radio
suggestions.note: Dave Sitek's own band — he produced nearly every YYY album while making this art-rock landmark with David Bowie guesting on 'Province'

suggestions.label: Embryonic (2009) — The Flaming Lips
suggestions.note: Karen O contributed backing vocals, screaming animal sounds, and noises to three tracks — the Lips' most abrasive, experimental album

suggestions.label: The Seer (2012) — Swans
suggestions.note: Karen O sings 'Song for a Warrior' on Michael Gira's monumental 2-hour opus — a surprising and beautiful vocal appearance

suggestions.label: Crazy Clown Time (2011) — David Lynch
suggestions.note: Karen O provides vocals on 'Pinky's Dream' — the lead single from David Lynch's debut music album, as strange and gorgeous as you'd expect

suggestions.label: Crush Songs (2014) — Karen O
suggestions.note: Karen O's intimate solo debut — lo-fi bedroom recordings from 2006-2007, a stark contrast to her ferocious live persona

suggestions.label: Broken Bells (2010) — Broken Bells
suggestions.note: Danger Mouse's other great collaboration — with James Mercer of The Shins. If you like Lux Prima's dreamy production, this is the next step

suggestions.label: Cool It Down (2022) — Yeah Yeah Yeahs
suggestions.note: YYY's comeback after 9 years of silence — produced again by Dave Sitek, featuring 'Spitting Off the Edge of the World' with Perfume Genius

suggestions.label: It's Blitz! (2009) — Yeah Yeah Yeahs
suggestions.note: YYY's synth-forward reinvention produced by Nick Launay and Dave Sitek — home to 'Heads Will Roll' and 'Zero'

play_commands.title: Exploring: Nas
play_commands.narrative: You came to Nas through "I Can" off God's Son — a track that samples Beethoven's "Für Elise" and was produced by Salaam Remi, one of Nas's most trusted collaborators who also produced Amy Winehouse's Back to Black. Illmatic (1994) was built by a dream team of boom-bap architects — DJ Premier, Pete Rock, Q-Tip, and Large Professor — and that production lineage connects Nas to virtually every corner of '90s East Coast hip-hop. His recent run with Hit-Boy across six albums (King's Disease through Magic 3) won him his first Grammy, while his long-awaited 2025 collaboration with DJ Premier, Light-Years, closed a loop decades in the making. Nas's collaboration web runs deep: he was part of The Firm supergroup with AZ and Foxy Brown (produced by Dr. Dre), guested on Mobb Deep's The Infamous and Raekwon's Only Built 4 Cuban Linx, made Distant Relatives with Damian Marley, and his father Olu Dara — a jazz musician — played on Illmatic itself.

suggestions.label: Illmatic (1994) — Nas
suggestions.note: Produced by DJ Premier, Pete Rock, Q-Tip, and Large Professor — the album that defined East Coast hip-hop and is in the Library of Congress

suggestions.label: Light-Years (2025) — Nas & DJ Premier
suggestions.note: The long-awaited full collaboration album with DJ Premier, who produced "N.Y. State of Mind" and "Nas Is Like" — decades of chemistry distilled into one record

suggestions.label: King's Disease (2020) — Nas
suggestions.note: Entirely produced by Hit-Boy, won Nas his first Grammy for Best Rap Album after 13 nominations — launched a six-album run together

suggestions.label: Distant Relatives (2010) — Nas & Damian Marley
suggestions.note: Collaborative album with Bob Marley's son blending hip-hop and reggae, royalties donated to build schools in Africa

suggestions.label: The Infamous (1995) — Mobb Deep
suggestions.note: Nas guests on "Eye for an Eye" — fellow Queensbridge legends who shared the same streets and production circle with DJ Premier

suggestions.label: Doe or Die (1995) — AZ
suggestions.note: AZ debuted on Illmatic's "Life's a Bitch" and this solo album was his response — they later formed The Firm supergroup together

suggestions.label: Only Built 4 Cuban Linx... (1995) — Raekwon
suggestions.note: Nas guests on "Verbal Intercourse" alongside Ghostface — the mafioso rap classic that paralleled It Was Written's Escobar era

suggestions.label: The Album (1997) — The Firm
suggestions.note: Supergroup with Nas, AZ, Foxy Brown, and Nature — produced by Dr. Dre on Aftermath Entertainment, a rare East-West crossover

play_commands.title: Exploring: Sarah Davachi
play_commands.narrative: Sarah Davachi is a new arrival in your library — you're hearing Antiphonals, her 2021 Mellotron-and-organ album on Late Music, the label she founded as an imprint of Warp Records. She's a Mills College–trained composer and UCLA musicologist who's been compared to Éliane Radigue and Terry Riley for her patient, psychoacoustic drone work. Beyond her solo catalog, she's collaborated with Ariel Kalma (the legendary French synthesist) on Intemporel and with Sean McCann on Mother of Pearl. She's also worked alongside Grouper, William Basinski, and Oren Ambarchi — all part of the same West Coast experimental orbit. Her biggest sonic leap is between Antiphonals (Mellotron textures) and Cantus, Descant (recorded on six pipe organs across Europe), and her most recent work, The Head As Form'd In The Crier's Choir, pushes deeper into chamber territory.

suggestions.label: Cantus, Descant (2020) — Sarah Davachi
suggestions.note: Recorded on six different pipe organs across North America and Europe — her most ambitious work and a major shift from the Mellotron textures of Antiphonals

suggestions.label: Gave in Rest (2018) — Sarah Davachi
suggestions.note: Described by NYT's Jon Pareles as 'an arc of mourning and mysticism' — recorded in European churches and lapidariums during her travels

suggestions.label: Let Night Come On Bells End The Day (2018) — Sarah Davachi
suggestions.note: Her Recital Records album — meditative and hypnotic, bridging her early electronic work with the acoustic direction that led to Antiphonals

suggestions.label: The Head As Form'd In The Crier's Choir (2024) — Sarah Davachi
suggestions.note: Her most recent double album on Late Music — pushes deeper into chamber ensemble writing with strings, woodwinds, and brass alongside electronics

suggestions.label: Intemporel (2019) — Sarah Davachi & Ariel Kalma
suggestions.note: Collaboration with Ariel Kalma, the French synth/flute pioneer active since the 1970s — merges Davachi's drones with Kalma's cosmic textures

suggestions.label: Mother of Pearl (2021) — Sarah Davachi & Sean McCann
suggestions.note: Collaboration with Sean McCann (Recital Records founder) — a warm, intimate meeting of two West Coast ambient minds

suggestions.label: Trilogie De La Mort (1998) — Éliane Radigue
suggestions.note: The Stranger called Davachi 'the closest thing we have to a hybrid of Éliane Radigue and Terry Riley' — this is Radigue's masterwork, three hours of ARP 2500 drone

suggestions.label: All Life Long (2024) — Kali Malone
suggestions.note: Fellow organ-focused composer working in sustained tones and just intonation — shares Davachi's interest in psychoacoustic phenomena and pipe organ resonance

play_commands.title: Fripp as Pop Producer
play_commands.context: Exploring Robert Fripp's production work beyond King Crimson — specifically his rare pop-adjacent productions
play_commands.narrative:
Robert Fripp built his legend on King Crimson's angular prog and Frippertronics ambient tape loops. But between 1977 and 1982, he took a detour into surprisingly accessible territory — producing records for artists with genuinely pop sensibilities.

It started with **The Roches' self-titled debut (1979)** — three sisters from New Jersey singing gorgeous folk harmonies. Fripp heard something in their voices and gave them a warm, idiosyncratic production that let the harmonies breathe while adding his unmistakable guitar textures. It remains arguably the most human, warm thing he ever put his name on.

He liked it enough to come back for **Keep On Doing (1982)**, their second album — same folk-pop magic, Fripp on guitar and "devices." If you loved the debut, this is the direct sequel.

In between, Fripp produced **Daryl Hall's Sacred Songs (recorded 1977, released 1980)** — yes, *that* Daryl Hall, of Hall & Oates. Hall's blue-eyed soul voice swimming in Frippertronics and art-rock textures. RCA shelved it for three years because it wasn't the pop hit machine they expected. A fascinating hybrid that starts from a pop songwriter's DNA.

He also produced **Peter Gabriel 2: Scratch (1978)** — Gabriel was writing accessible, almost pop songs ("D.I.Y.", "On the Air") but Fripp gave them an angular, unsettling edge. More accessible than King Crimson, weirder than The Roches.

His own **Exposure (1979)** is described as his "most pop-oriented" solo work — quirky pop songs and spiky rockers featuring vocals from Hall and Gabriel. It forms an unofficial trilogy with Sacred Songs and Gabriel 2, all recorded in the same period with overlapping personnel.

After 1982, Fripp retreated back to ambient/art-rock territory — Sylvian collaborations, reformed King Crimson, Soundscapes. The Roches really were the sweet spot where the avant-garde wizard and the pop world briefly overlapped.

suggestions.label: ▶ The Roches — The Roches (1979)
suggestions.note: The debut that started it all — folk harmonies meet Frippertronics. The warmest thing Fripp ever produced.

suggestions.label: ▶ The Roches — Keep On Doing (1982)
suggestions.note: The direct sequel — same folk-pop harmony magic, Fripp on guitar and devices.

suggestions.label: ▶ Daryl Hall — Sacred Songs (1977)
suggestions.note: Hall & Oates' Daryl Hall goes art-rock with Fripp producing. RCA shelved it for 3 years — too weird for pop, too pop for prog.

suggestions.label: ▶ Peter Gabriel — Scratch (1978)
suggestions.note: Gabriel writing accessible pop songs, Fripp making them angular and unsettling. The sweet spot.

suggestions.label: ▶ Robert Fripp — Exposure (1979)
suggestions.note: Fripp's most pop-oriented solo work — vocals from Hall and Gabriel. The unofficial trilogy with Sacred Songs and Scratch.

play_commands.title: Exploring: Robert Fripp
play_commands.narrative: Robert Fripp is new territory for you — you came here via The Roches, whose self-titled 1979 debut Fripp produced. You already listen to several artists in Fripp's orbit: Brian Eno (3 plays), Peter Gabriel, David Bowie, and David Byrne — all of whom Fripp collaborated with extensively. From his pioneering ambient guitar loops with Eno to leading King Crimson through five decades of reinvention, Fripp sits at a nexus of artists you already love.

suggestions.label: In The Court Of The Crimson King — King Crimson
suggestions.note: The 1969 debut that invented prog rock — essential starting point for King Crimson

suggestions.label: Discipline — King Crimson
suggestions.note: The 1981 new-wave-era KC with Adrian Belew — if you like Talking Heads, start here

suggestions.label: Red — King Crimson
suggestions.note: Heavy, intense 1974 power trio album — widely considered KC's hardest-hitting record

suggestions.label: Evening Star — Robert Fripp & Brian Eno
suggestions.note: Gorgeous ambient collaboration with Eno — the sequel to No Pussyfooting

suggestions.label: Exposure — Robert Fripp
suggestions.note: Fripp's solo debut featuring Peter Gabriel and Daryl Hall — art-rock at its most adventurous

suggestions.label: The First Day — David Sylvian & Robert Fripp
suggestions.note: Dark, atmospheric 1993 collaboration with Japan's David Sylvian

play_commands.title: Exploring: The Roches
play_commands.narrative: The Roches are a folk trio of sisters — Maggie, Terre, and Suzzy Roche — known for their tight harmonies and witty songwriting. Hammond Song, from their acclaimed 1979 debut produced by Robert Fripp, is one of their signature tracks. This is your first recorded listen — a perfect entry point to explore their catalog, which spans from the late '70s through 2007.

suggestions.label: The Roches — The Roches
suggestions.note: Their 1979 debut — home of Hammond Song and the album that started it all.

suggestions.label: Nurds — The Roches
suggestions.note: Their 1980 follow-up, another Robert Fripp production with inventive arrangements.

suggestions.label: Keep On Doing — The Roches
suggestions.note: 1982 album showing the sisters at their most playful and harmonically adventurous.

suggestions.label: Seductive Reasoning — Maggie and Terre Roche
suggestions.note: The pre-Roches 1975 album by Maggie and Terre — raw folk origins before Suzzy joined.

suggestions.label: Moonswept — The Roches
suggestions.note: Their 2007 final studio album — mature songwriting and still gorgeous harmonies.

suggestions.label: The Roches Best (playlist)
suggestions.note: Community-curated playlist of Roches highlights — good overview of their career.

play_commands.title: Exploring: The Stranglers
play_commands.narrative: This is your first recorded listen to The Stranglers — you landed on "Golden Brown" from their 1981 album La Folie. The Stranglers are a British punk/new wave band known for their keyboard-driven sound, with a catalog stretching from 1977's Rattus Norvegicus through the early '90s. La Folie is one of their most acclaimed records, blending post-punk aggression with harpsichord-laced pop on tracks like "Golden Brown."

suggestions.label: Rattus Norvegicus — The Stranglers
suggestions.note: 12-track album from 1977.

suggestions.label: La Folie — The Stranglers
suggestions.note: 17-track album from 1981.

suggestions.label: Black and White — The Stranglers
suggestions.note: 18-track album from 1978.

suggestions.label: No More Heroes — The Stranglers
suggestions.note: 14-track album from 1977.

suggestions.label: BEST OF THE STRANGLERS 🖤
suggestions.note: Community-curated mix of The Stranglers and related artists.
